definition: A type of financing where a business uses its outstanding invoices as collateral to receive immediate cash.
An electronic funds transfer system used by MCA companies to withdraw daily or weekly payments directly from a business bank account.
The total cash amount provided to a business by an MCA provider, which must be repaid with the factor rate applied.
The annualized cost of borrowing expressed as a percentage. MCAs do not technically have an APR since they are not loans, but an equivalent APR can be calculated for comparison purposes.
A third-party intermediary who connects businesses with MCA providers. Brokers earn commissions and may or may not disclose all available options.
A legal document signed by the business owner allowing the MCA company to obtain a court judgment without trial if the business defaults. Banned in some states.
The fixed or percentage-based amount withheld from daily revenues to repay an MCA.
Failure to meet the repayment terms of an MCA agreement. Can trigger additional fees, legal action, or UCC lien enforcement.
When an MCA provider takes two payments in a single day, either intentionally or due to a processing error.
A multiplier (typically 1.1 to 1.5) applied to the advance amount to determine the total repayment. A factor rate of 1.3 on a $50,000 advance means $65,000 total repayment.
The percentage of daily credit card sales or revenue retained by the MCA company as repayment. Typically ranges from 10% to 25%.
A financing option where a business receives a lump sum in exchange for a percentage of future credit card sales or revenue. Technically a purchase of future receivables, not a loan.
The percentage of daily sales that is automatically deducted to repay the MCA. Same as holdback percentage.
A strategy where a new MCA is used to pay off multiple existing MCAs, consolidating them into a single payment.
Taking out multiple MCAs simultaneously from different providers. Considered high-risk as it multiplies daily payment obligations.
A legal filing that gives the MCA provider a lien on business assets. Used as collateral and recorded publicly.
